frames.description: ground truth — what the narration presents as simply true
the bearer, who carries the wend-pipe between the two ages

branches.description: the planted future world-line forking at sc-26 (sc-27p..sc-40p)

entities.description: the worn stone steps to the upper spring's catch-basin
the lower farmland by the river crossing
the basin's lower vent where the overflow runs back into the rock
the stone Halden cut to mark the firm ford
the slow-growing sapling that can bridge the gorge over generations
the carved bone whistle that moves the bearer between the ages
the deep catch-basin fed by the upper spring

predicates.description: the state a thing is in (e.g. rotting / sealed / clean)

facts.claim: Brae Hollow is young and loud with living: the spring runs full and quick, the craft-row clatters, and the river crossing is thick with carts and folk.
Sera tends the upper spring and the stone catch-basin at the Cistern Steps; the whole low hollow drinks what comes down off these Steps.
Sera says the Steps are hers to mind and have been since she could carry a pail, and the whole low hollow drinks what comes down off them.
At the basin's lower vent Sera finds a creeping rot working its slow way into the stone; left alone it would foul everything that runs past it and turn the cistern bad.
Sera packs the lower vent course by course with clay, binding and fibre, sealing the rot off from the running water and the air; she runs her hand over the seal and says it holds.
Sera says the seal holds the rot, not for her to see how long, but for whoever keeps the Steps after she is gone — let them find it sound.
Sera cuts a small keeper's mark — three strokes and a notch — into the stone beside the sealed vent.
Sera says she cuts the mark so a later hand knows the rock was tended here and does not go digging at sound rock thinking it failed.
Once the work is done the cistern runs sweet: the rot is sealed away below and the flow above runs clean and untroubled; the water tastes of nothing but itself.
Halden is the boundary-mason in the Loom Quarter; he is the one folk go to when a line needs settling, and he marks the edges the quarter lives by.
A dispute breaks open over which river crossing the dye-carts may use, the heavy carts churning the soft fords to bog; it comes to Halden to settle.
Reading the river, Halden finds one stretch where the bed is bone under the gravel; the channel can wander but this one crossing stays firm, though no one has marked it.
Halden says the river shifts its bed year on year and most fords will be gone or drowned a generation on, but here the bed is bone and this one stays firm.
Halden squares up and cuts a boundary-stone to mark the firm ford, closing the cart-dispute in stone.
Halden carries the stone down and beds it deep and true at the head of the firm ford — the one crossing that will hold firm when the river shifts its bed — named now in stone at its head.
Halden set the stone for a craft-row squabble over cart-tracks and nothing larger; drought never crosses his mind.
The Wend Fields are the lower farmland, green and worked, good ground in a good year, and every year here is a good year now.
The bearer comes into the young hollow at the Wend Fields, carrying a carved bone wend-pipe on a cord at the chest.
The bearer watches Sera pack the lower vent and seal the rot away, filing it as a woman mending a spring on an ordinary morning, with no weight on it yet.
The bearer watches Halden bed his boundary-stone at the head of the firm ford, seeing only a mason settling a cart-dispute with a marked stone.
The wend-pipe moves the bearer between the two ages on the very spot it is played: stand on this ground young, play, and stand on this same ground old — one whistle, one place, two times.
At the crossing a dry gorge cuts the way to the far field across the channel; folk do not cross it and the far field lies idle for want of a way over.

A field-elder gives the bearer a slow-growing gorge-sapling, saying that given the lip of the cleft and time enough it will throw a root each side and grow itself into the gap, becoming a living bridge generations on.

Playing the pipe at the crossing, the bearer stands a breath in the age to come: the green gone, the river thinned, the ground parched, and the spring up the slope down to a thread, the valley near its last water.

The young hollow is the old hollow's cause: the two ages are one place before and after, and whatever is done now is done to the people of the later age — what is packed, marked, planted or left undone lands generations on.

The bearer weighs whether to plant the sapling: it will do nothing for this green age and be no bridge in any lifetime they will meet — to plant it is to do a thing for people they will never know against a withering not yet believed.
The choice stands at the Wend crossing: plant the gorge-sapling at the lip of the cleft, or wrap it up and carry it off, letting the far field lie idle and the gorge stay a gorge.

Three generations on, Brae Hollow lies under a long drought, grey and quiet, the riverbed mostly stone, and the spring at the head of the valley down to a thread — the last water in the valley near enough.
At the Cistern Steps a man named Marn keeps the cistern, the last good water in the valley; every other spring has soured or gone dry while his still gives.
Marn does not know why this cistern alone runs clean while the whole valley's water dies; the why of it is a thing his age has lost, and he has half a dozen guesses he trusts none of.

Crossed over to the dry age, the bearer recognises the basin's lower vent as the same vent watched packed and sealed three generations gone; Marn sees only the place the water runs back into the rock.
Three generations into the withering, with every other spring soured or dead, the cistern off these Steps runs clean and cold and good; Marn draws it and it is sweet.
At the lower vent the bearer finds in the old grey stone a keeper's mark — three strokes and a notch, worn but plain — set by someone the late age does not remember; Marn has never noticed it.
The Loom Quarter has gone silent; one loom still works, where Pell the weaver, the last of the trade, throws a shuttle on the dead craft-row.
Pell knows only that there was once a dispute over the crossings and the carts — a half-remembered word — but knows nothing of who settled it or cut any stone; the reasons have washed out.

Ode the field-warden keeps the crossing now, working out where the Withering folk can cross dry-shod to the ground that will still grow, the channel different every season as it shifts in its dying bed.

Working the shifted channel, Ode digs up an old boundary-stone stranded in the silt and reads its cutting: a mark of the firm ford, a crossing named to hold; reading it against the channel Ode sees it points true to where the bed is bone.
Ode does not know who set the stone or when — the cutting is in an old hand and says nothing of its maker — but says 'Somebody knew this water'; that is as far as the knowing goes.

On the firm ford the old stone marks, the bed holds bone-hard under the shifting silt and the Withering folk cross dry-shod to the last green field; the crossing holds while the river dies around it, the late age taking it on faith with the giver forgotten.

Across the channel, spanning the dry gorge, there is a bridge-tree: a slow gorge-sapling that threw a root each side of the cleft and over the long years grew itself into a living span the Withering folk cross dry over the gorge.

Tamsin the far-field settler crosses the bridge-tree each day to the one good ground left, coaxing a crop from the far field the gorge once cut off; without the far ground and the bridge the hollow would have starved.

Crossing back to the Founding morning, the bearer watches Sera find the rot at the lower vent and pack it tight and cut her mark — and the prose states: this is why the cistern runs clean three generations on, the seal under all that good water.

The bearer watches Halden read the dying river before it dies, judge the one firm ford and bed his stone at its head for a cart-dispute — and the prose states: this is why the ford holds, the cause Marn could not name and Ode could only guess at, now seen plainly.

Brae Hollow withers but is not lost: the water holds off the Steps clean to the last, the ford holds on the old stone's word, and the bridge-tree holds spanning the gorge to the green field — what was done on a bright forgotten morning holds the dry age up.
